title: BNEF Style Report Template
text:
Which PJM and ERCOT regions show persistent price separation?
How much of that spread appears to reflect transmission congestion?
Do high-renewable and low-net-load periods coincide with abnormal spread or negative pricing?
What does this imply for transmission, storage, and modernization priorities?





Where are the most persistent nodal and zonal spreads?
Which nodes repeatedly rank near the top of congestion exposure?
How does PJM compare with ERCOT in spread persistence?





How closely do large spreads coincide with high congestion components?
Where does spread appear to be a strong congestion proxy?
Where should spread be interpreted more cautiously?





During high-renewable periods, does congestion intensify or ease?
During low-net-load periods, do negative prices become more frequent?
Which regions appear structurally sensitive to renewable buildout?





Which patterns suggest a transmission bottleneck worth upgrading?
Where could storage help absorb oversupply or reduce spread volatility?
What broader modernization priorities emerge across the two markets?





Raw market data sources
Cleaning and harmonization rules
Congestion and spread metrics
Limits of interpreting LMP spread as a congestion proxy

title: PJM Methodology
text:
Core data fields
The harmonized schema centers on:

timestamp
node_id
node_name
lmp
energy
congestion
loss
optional renewable_mw, load_mw, zone, latitude, longitude

title: PJM Methodology
text:
Congestion metrics








Metric
Purpose




abs_congestion
Direct congestion intensity


price_separation
Market-wide node divergence in a given interval


hub_lmp
Reference price used for node-to-hub comparisons


node_to_hub_spread
Local divergence from a configured market reference


persistent_congestion_score
Composite score for map prominence and screening


negative_price_flag
Abnormal pricing indicator


renewable_penetration
Renewable share proxy when load is available


net_load_mw
Load minus renewable generation

title: PJM Methodology
text:
Hub reference logic
node_to_hub_spread is now configuration-driven.
The pipeline first checks config/markets.yml for explicit PJM hub-reference rules. If a configured match is available, it is used as the reference LMP. If not, the code falls back to a heuristic hub detection rule and then, if needed, to market mean.
This hierarchy keeps the site usable with sample data while making the research definition of “hub” explicit and auditable.

title: PJM Methodology
text:
Publication note
This site is built with Quarto, but the interactive map HTML is generated by the Python pipeline. Quarto then copies those map artifacts into the published website.

title: Schema Checks
text: Use this notebook to inspect raw PJM and ERCOT files and confirm they can be mapped into the shared nodal schema.

title: ERCOT Exploration
text: Investigate nodal congestion, price spreads, and renewable-linked stress periods in ERCOT.

title: Final Report
text:
ERCOT: median nodal price separation is 10.40, median node-to-hub spread is 3.95, and average absolute congestion is 7.28 across 2 intervals.
PJM: median nodal price separation is 8.50, median node-to-hub spread is 2.15, and average absolute congestion is 6.45 across 2 intervals.
ERCOT: West Export (ERCOT_B) is the top recurring congestion-exposed node with average absolute congestion of 12.90.
PJM: Central Interface (PJM_B) is the top recurring congestion-exposed node with average absolute congestion of 11.40.





ERCOT: average absolute congestion is 7.28 while median price separation is 10.40 and median node-to-hub spread is 3.95, suggesting spread and congestion move together in the sample.
PJM: average absolute congestion is 6.45 while median price separation is 8.50 and median node-to-hub spread is 2.15, suggesting spread and congestion move together in the sample.
Spread is treated here as a congestion signal candidate rather than a definitive measure, because losses and market design effects can also contribute.





ERCOT: high-renewable intervals show 0.83 lower average absolute congestion than low-renewable intervals.
PJM: high-renewable intervals show 2.17 lower average absolute congestion than low-renewable intervals.
ERCOT: negative price frequency is 0.0% in the current sample.
PJM: negative price frequency is 0.0% in the current sample.
Stress-event intervals remain useful candidates for deeper low-net-load and renewable interaction checks:
ERCOT 2025-07-01T00:00:00+00:00: mean absolute congestion 7.70, price separation 8.40.
PJM 2025-07-01T00:00:00+00:00: mean absolute congestion 7.53, price separation 7.60.





Persistent high-spread nodes are candidate locations for transmission reinforcement or operational bottleneck review.
If future runs show repeated spread during renewable-rich intervals, those regions become stronger candidates for storage deployment.
Sustained nodal divergence across time would support broader grid modernization investment where renewable growth is outrunning transfer capability.

title: Grid Congestion in PJM
text:
Research website
This site publishes the PJM-focused output of the us-grid-congestion-monitor pipeline. It uses nodal electricity price data to study how renewable buildout interacts with transmission constraints in PJM and what those patterns may imply for transmission investment, storage deployment, and grid modernization.
The project is organized around four PJM questions:
::: :::

title: Grid Congestion in PJM
text:
What this site contains


A narrative summary of the four core research questions
Interactive PJM congestion maps
A node-to-hub spread animation layer
Methodology notes on harmonization, hub references, and congestion metrics
Download links for map-ready and report-ready exports

title: Grid Congestion in PJM
text:
Current project framing
The published analysis pipeline follows this sequence:

Download PJM raw ISO data through gridstatus
Clean PJM market-specific schema
Harmonize PJM nodal observations into the analytical frame
Build congestion, spread, renewable, and hub-reference features
Generate charts, maps, export packs, and a report


The website assumes the latest site artifacts are generated by the Python pipeline before publication. On GitHub Pages, Quarto renders the site shell while existing map HTML and CSV exports are copied into the published output.

title: Grid Congestion in PJM
text:
{#findings .section-anchor}Key Findings
This project is designed around four analytical questions rather than around raw pipeline stages. That matters because the same metric can play different roles depending on whether we are diagnosing a PJM bottleneck, testing a hypothesis about renewable buildout, or framing a transmission implication.


1. Persistent price separation
Core indicators:

price_separation
abs_node_to_hub_spread
persistent_congestion_score



2. Spread as congestion signal
Core indicators:

avg_abs_congestion
congestion_share_of_lmp
overlap between large spread and high congestion intervals



3. Renewable and net-load stress
Core indicators:

renewable_penetration
net_load_mw
negative_price_flag



4. Investment implications
Core interpretation:

transmission upgrade candidates
storage siting opportunities
modernization priorities where divergence persists



The site treats spread as a candidate congestion signal, not as automatic proof of congestion. A large node-to-hub spread becomes more convincing when it coincides with elevated congestion components, persistence across intervals, and recurring stress conditions in the same location.

title: Grid Congestion in PJM
text:
{#maps .section-anchor}Interactive Maps

PJM congestion map




Node-to-hub spread animation




Use the toggle in the main maps to switch between congestion intensity and node-to-hub spread.
Bubble size reflects a persistent congestion score rather than a single-interval spike.
Hover panels show zone, spread, congestion, negative-price frequency, and hub-reference source.
The animation layer helps distinguish structural bottlenecks from short-lived price events.

Pipeline design

Core data fields
The harmonized schema centers on:

Congestion metrics








Metric
Purpose




abs_congestion
Direct congestion intensity


price_separation
Market-wide node divergence in a given interval


hub_lmp
Reference price used for node-to-hub comparisons


node_to_hub_spread
Local divergence from a configured market reference


persistent_congestion_score
Composite score for map prominence and screening


negative_price_flag
Abnormal pricing indicator


renewable_penetration
Renewable share proxy when load is available


net_load_mw
Load minus renewable generation






Hub reference logic
node_to_hub_spread is configuration-driven. The pipeline first checks config/markets.yml for explicit PJM hub-reference rules. If a configured match is available, it is used as the reference LMP. If not, the code falls back to heuristic hub detection and then, if needed, to market mean.

title: Grid Congestion Analysis Report
text:
ERCOT: average absolute congestion was 7.28, with median nodal price separation of 10.40 across 2 intervals.
PJM: average absolute congestion was 6.45, with median nodal price separation of 8.50 across 2 intervals.







West Export (ERCOT_B) ranked #1 with average absolute congestion of 12.90.
Houston Load (ERCOT_C) ranked #2 with average absolute congestion of 6.45.
North Hub (ERCOT_A) ranked #3 with average absolute congestion of 2.50. ### PJM
Central Interface (PJM_B) ranked #1 with average absolute congestion of 11.40.
West Hub (PJM_A) ranked #2 with average absolute congestion of 5.05.
Coastal Pocket (PJM_C) ranked #3 with average absolute congestion of 2.90.






ERCOT: high-renewable intervals showed 0.83 lower average absolute congestion than low-renewable intervals.
PJM: high-renewable intervals showed 2.17 lower average absolute congestion than low-renewable intervals.

title: PJM Findings
text:
Reading the maps
The PJM congestion maps are intentionally product-like. Bubble size tracks a persistent congestion score, while the color layer can be switched between:

average absolute congestion
average absolute node-to-hub spread

Hover panels are designed to answer the practical question: why is this node worth looking at?

title: PJM Findings
text:
Narrative interpretation
The site treats spread as a candidate congestion signal, not as automatic proof of congestion. A large node-to-hub spread becomes more convincing when it coincides with:

elevated congestion components in LMP
persistence across intervals
recurring stress conditions in the same location

That framing helps separate structural bottlenecks from one-off price events.

title: PJM Findings
text:
Latest generated report
The latest PJM pipeline report is available as a raw artifact here: final_report.md

title: Final Report
text:
ERCOT: median nodal price separation is 10.40, median node-to-hub spread is 3.95, and average absolute congestion is 7.28 across 2 intervals.
PJM: median nodal price separation is 8.50, median node-to-hub spread is 2.15, and average absolute congestion is 6.45 across 2 intervals.
ERCOT: West Export (ERCOT_B) is the top recurring congestion-exposed node with average absolute congestion of 12.90.
PJM: Central Interface (PJM_B) is the top recurring congestion-exposed node with average absolute congestion of 11.40.





ERCOT: average absolute congestion is 7.28 while median price separation is 10.40 and median node-to-hub spread is 3.95, suggesting spread and congestion move together in the sample.
PJM: average absolute congestion is 6.45 while median price separation is 8.50 and median node-to-hub spread is 2.15, suggesting spread and congestion move together in the sample.
Spread is treated here as a congestion signal candidate rather than a definitive measure, because losses and market design effects can also contribute.





ERCOT: high-renewable intervals show 0.83 lower average absolute congestion than low-renewable intervals.
PJM: high-renewable intervals show 2.17 lower average absolute congestion than low-renewable intervals.
ERCOT: negative price frequency is 0.0% in the current sample.
PJM: negative price frequency is 0.0% in the current sample.
Stress-event intervals remain useful candidates for deeper low-net-load and renewable interaction checks:
ERCOT 2025-07-01T00:00:00+00:00: mean absolute congestion 7.70, price separation 8.40.
PJM 2025-07-01T00:00:00+00:00: mean absolute congestion 7.53, price separation 7.60.





Persistent high-spread nodes are candidate locations for transmission reinforcement or operational bottleneck review.
If future runs show repeated spread during renewable-rich intervals, those regions become stronger candidates for storage deployment.
Sustained nodal divergence across time would support broader grid modernization investment where renewable growth is outrunning transfer capability.
